sourceMovie.title: Vertigo
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Vertigo (1958) stuck because it commits to its mystery premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Alfred Hitchcock's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The mystery and romance blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Rebecca
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where Vertigo excels in mystery, Rebecca (1940) doubles down on romance to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: Night Train to Lisbon
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Night Train to Lisbon (2013) is the natural next step after Vertigo: same mystery DNA, different perspective. The tense streak and romance elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: Dead Again
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of Vertigo who respond to its mystery will find Dead Again (1991) immediately familiar. Both films share a romance sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: Twelve Monkeys
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason Vertigo fans keep landing on Twelve Monkeys (1995) — the mystery is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: The Game
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Game draws from the same mystery well as Vertigo, channeling thriller in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 1997 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: Trance
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Trance (2013) captures the mystery appeal of Vertigo without simply imitating it. The thriller streak running through both films is what puts Trance at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: I'm Thinking of Ending Things
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The mystery appeal of Vertigo — that tense edge, the thriller — translates cleanly into I'm Thinking of Ending Things (2020). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: The Maltese Falcon
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind The Maltese Falcon (1941) clearly understood what makes mystery work — the same quality that keeps Vertigo rewatchable. Expect the same dark rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: Greta
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Greta (2019) is the kind of film Vertigo fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The mystery overlap is real, but it's the tense emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

recommendations.title: Dressed to Kill
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Dressed to Kill shares Vertigo's gift for mystery — the 1980 film delivers thriller without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

faq.question: What should I watch after Vertigo?
faq.answer: Try Rebecca, Night Train to Lisbon, Dead Again—each shares DNA with Vertigo while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Vertigo?
faq.answer: Vertigo (1958) is best described as Mystery, Romance, Thriller. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Vertigo worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 8.1/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this mystery lane.
